purpose: Separates directional demographic claims (typically 7-9/10 confidence) from magnitude/timing claims (typically 3-6/10 confidence) to prevent false precision in portfolio decisions.
components: Directional assessment: which way does aging push the variable? (high confidence, 7-9/10)
Magnitude assessment: by how much? (low confidence, 3-6/10)
Timing assessment: when do thresholds cross? (moderate confidence, varies by variable)
Calibration check: does the confidence interval allow actionable precision?
Portfolio sizing rule: size for directional conviction, not magnitude precision
current_assessment: The single most important methodological finding. Every debate pair independently converged on this split. All specific quantitative claims (14/14 hit rate, 40/35/15/10 decomposition, 60/25/10/5 China scenarios, 35-40% fiscal stress probability) either have CIs too wide for decisions or rest on fragile identification.
limitations: Does not help distinguish between directional claims that are independently validated vs. those that are derivatives of a single data source
Some portfolio decisions require magnitude estimates even when confidence is low
The framework itself does not provide guidance on how to size positions given only directional conviction

purpose: Distinguishes between financial repression with demographic tailwinds (successful deleveraging) and without (Japan-style stasis), to assess likely outcomes for current aging economies.
components: Channel 1: Inflation erosion of real debt value (~30-50% historical contribution)
Channel 2: Nominal GDP growth diluting debt/GDP ratio (~25-40% historical contribution)
Channel 3: Primary surpluses (~10-20% historical contribution)
Channel 4: Direct repression mechanisms: rate ceilings, captive buyer mandates (~5-15% historical contribution)
Demographic tailwind test: Is labor force growth sufficient to power Channel 2?
current_assessment: US at 0.3% labor force growth is structurally closer to Japan pattern (stasis) than 1946-1980 pattern (successful deleveraging). Repression is the path of least resistance but without growth, the likelier outcome is prolonged moderate negative real returns without debt resolution — unless AI fills the growth gap.
limitations: Four channels interact and are endogenous — precise decomposition is unidentifiable
Specific percentage allocations across channels were refuted as unfounded
Reserve currency status may change the dynamics in ways not captured by historical cases
AI productivity offset could alter the growth channel contribution

purpose: Provides an inductive template for the progression of aging economies through financial and fiscal phases, useful as organizing heuristic for identifying where an economy currently sits.
components: Phase 1: Early aging signals, growing awareness, initial policy response
Phase 2: Accelerating dependency, fiscal pressures mounting, initial financial repression
Phase 3: Full fiscal accommodation, monetary-fiscal coordination
Phase 4: Structural adjustment, potential crisis dynamics
Phase 5: New equilibrium (Japan terminal state)
current_assessment: US placed in Phase 2. Japan has traversed all five phases. However, 2/6 historical episodes deviate substantially (Germany never entered Phase 3, Italy determined by ECB). A taxonomy, not a law.
limitations: Inductively derived from only 6 episodes
2/6 episodes deviate substantially from the template
Does not account for unique institutional features (ECB, reserve currency, etc.)
US has features that may cause deviation from any historical template
Not falsifiable as a predictive framework

purpose: Identifies the drivers of stock-bond correlation regimes and assesses the current transition probability toward persistent positive correlation.
components: Primary driver: CPI level (>4% → 85% positive correlation; <2% → 30%)
Historical base rate: positive correlation governed 64% of post-war period
Four conditions for negative regime (all weakening): favorable demographics, globalization, credible disinflation, EM reserve accumulation
Feedback loop: positive correlation → lower hedge value → higher term premium → reinforces positive correlation
Persistence test: 25-40% base rate for >5-year positive correlation regimes
Institutional lag: portfolio models update on 3-5 year mandate review cycles
current_assessment: Composite macro configuration favors positive correlation (6-7/10). Demographics is one input operating through the inflation channel but has no identifiable independent effect. The persistence probability (25-40% for >5 years) means the transition is far from guaranteed despite directional conviction.
limitations: Demographics not statistically significant after controlling for CPI
Persistence of positive correlation regime is uncertain (25-40% base rate)
CPI trajectory itself depends on the unresolved aging-inflation/deflation binary
The feedback loop mechanism has not been quantitatively calibrated

purpose: Maps the demographic-to-inflation transmission channel in emerging markets through CPI food-weight asymmetry, export bans, and political instability.
components: CPI food-weight asymmetry: identical price shocks produce 3-10x larger CPI impacts in EM vs. DM
Population growth concentration in high-food-weight countries (~800M by 2050)
Export ban feedback loop: food inflation → aging voter pressure → export restrictions → further price increases
Political instability transmission: Arab Spring confirmed food-price → instability mechanism
Agricultural labor bifurcation: capital-rich vs capital-poor regions increasing concentration risk
current_assessment: The arithmetic of CPI food-weight asymmetry is irrefutable and represents the clearest novel micro-foundation for EM sovereign risk. Forward projection depends on whether productivity gains (precision agriculture, alternative proteins) offset supply constraints. The bulk commodity supply constraint was partially refuted — binding primarily for labor-intensive crops, not mechanized grain/soybean.
limitations: Forward productivity gains could offset supply constraints
Bulk commodity supply constraint is overstated for mechanized crops
Export ban feedback depends on political systems and trade agreements
Biofuel mandate interaction is underexplored

purpose: Maps the three correlated channels through which immigration swings transmit to credit markets, identifying portfolio model blind spots.
components: Channel 1: RMBS — immigration affects housing demand, mortgage origination, and agency/non-agency pipeline
Channel 2: ABS — auto, student, and consumer credit volumes affected by population growth/decline
Channel 3: Leveraged borrower margins — labor availability directly affects operating costs for labor-intensive borrowers
Correlation structure: portfolio models treat channels as independent but they are correlated through common immigration driver
Curve shape prediction: restriction → front-end steepening (expectations), expansion → flattening
current_assessment: Immigration swing of ±2M/year (2x natural increase) makes this the highest-variance short-horizon demographic variable. Three-channel correlation creates stress that standard portfolio models underestimate. However, persistence rate is unknowable from n=6-7 episodes (CI [12%, 88%]).
limitations: Persistence rate unknowable from small sample (n=6-7)
Conflicting episode counts from different agents (1/7 vs 3/6)
Political volatility makes forward projection unreliable
Channel magnitudes not separately estimated
